cataloging_subject.label: The Disputisoun betwen the Bodi and the Soule
cataloging_subject.scope_note: One anonymous Middle English body-and-soul debate poem, beginning 'As i lay in a wynternyght'. The eight nested DIV2 elements in CME00101 are dialogue or scene sections within the poem, not eight additional works.

catalog_summary.title: The Disputisoun betwen the Bodi and the Soule
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Composed by the early fourteenth century at the latest; first attested in a late-thirteenth- or early-fourteenth-century witness
catalog_summary.region: England; earliest known witness associated with the West Midlands

titles.value: The Disputisoun betwen the Bodi and the Soule

assertion.notes: A cataloging title based on established Middle English forms; it is not asserted as an authorial title.

titles.value: A disputeson betwen the body and the sowle

scope.description: Rubric in Royal MS 18 A X and heading of Varnhagen's transcription

titles.value: As i lay in a wynternyght

scope.description: Opening words in the Royal-witness transcription

notes: DIMEV regularizes the incipit as 'As I lay in a winters night'; other witnesses and scholarship often supply the short designation 'Als I lay'.

titles.value: Explicit disputacio inter corpus et animam

scope.description: Latin colophon in Royal MS 18 A X

assertion.notes: The DIMEV v1.0 Royal-witness field gives a conflicting malformed explicit; the direct edition and current British Library catalogue agree on this reading.

agents.name: Hermann Varnhagen
notes: Editor, transcriber, collator, and commentator for the 1879 Anglia article from which CME00101 was encoded.

assertion.notes: No named Middle English author is supported. Historical attributions attached to the Latin Visio Philiberti are not transferred to this English adaptation.

scope.description: The Royal MS 18 A X transcription and apparatus published in Anglia 2 (1879), pp. 225-255
The Royal MS 18 A X text printed on pp. 229-244 and represented by CME00101
Variant comparison of the Royal text with the Laud, Auchinleck, Vernon, and Digby witnesses in the article's notes
German introduction and textual notes in the 1879 article; these passages are not included in CME00101

date_statements.display: By the early fourteenth century at the latest

assertion.notes: The late-thirteenth- or early-fourteenth-century Laud witness establishes circulation by this outer bound, but no authorial copy or exact composition date survives.

date_statements.display: Late thirteenth or early fourteenth century (Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108)

assertion.notes: This is the earliest currently identified witness among DIMEV's seven witnesses, not a composition date.

places.label: West Midlands, England
notes: Used only for the localization of the earliest known Laud witness, not asserted as the poem's place of composition.

assertion.notes: The English-language work is transmitted in English manuscript witnesses; this broad cultural origin does not localize its composition more narrowly.
The place statement describes the witness's reported production region. It is not evidence that the poem was composed there.

Short Latin speech labels and the explicit are embedded paratext and are not promoted to a whole-work language facet. DIMEV v1.0's record-level French/macaronic label reflects a French benediction unique to the collateral Laud witness and does not describe the Royal text encoded here.

form_statements.description: A 536-line stanzaic poem: 67 eight-line stanzas in the edition and manuscript catalogue, represented by 66 LG elements in the XML because one LG spans two stanzas.

assertion.notes: The narrator's nocturnal visionary frame introduces the dialogue.

summaries.value: A dreamer overhears a dead person's soul and body accuse one another of responsibility for sin. Their alternating speeches move from mutual reproach and the body's lament to the arrival of devils, the soul's appeal to Christ, and a warning to the living about death, judgment, and repentance.

CME00101 contains Varnhagen's Royal MS 18 A X transcription from its heading through 'Amen' and the explicit, printed on pp. 229-244. It excludes the article's German introduction on pp. 225-229 and the editorial notes and comparison with other witnesses that begin after the explicit on p. 244.

DIMEV 605 identifies seven manuscript witnesses of this Middle English work. They are witnesses or versions of the same poem, not seven independent related works. The Royal witness represented here has 67 eight-line stanzas; stanza IV was supplied in another hand on an inserted vellum strip now foliated 62.

extent.description: One poem of 536 verse lines in nine intellectual dialogue or scene sections. The XML has one DIV1, eight nested DIV2 elements, 66 LG elements, 536 L elements, and 16 PB elements. Varnhagen and the British Library catalogue count 67 eight-line stanzas; XML lines 433-448 are held in one 16-line LG and therefore reduce the encoded LG count by one.

content_parts.label: Opening vision and the soul's first accusation
content_parts.summary: The narrator sees a corpse at night; the soul addresses the body and blames its worldly appetites for their damnation.

content_parts.label: Respondit corpus ad animam
content_parts.summary: The body answers that the soul, endowed with reason, should have ruled it and is therefore culpable.

content_parts.label: Anima dicit ad corpus
content_parts.summary: The soul renews its accusation, dwelling on the body's vanity, luxury, and present corruption.

content_parts.label: Ait corpus ad animam
content_parts.summary: The body again charges the soul with failing to restrain bodily desire while both still had time to repent.

content_parts.label: Anima dicit ad corpus
content_parts.summary: The soul contrasts their former comforts with the body's grave and anticipates infernal punishment.

content_parts.label: Corpus ait ad animam
content_parts.summary: The body laments its decay and the separation from the soul.

content_parts.label: Anima dicit ad corpus
content_parts.summary: The soul rejects the body's excuses and reviews the moral failures that brought them to judgment.

content_parts.label: Demonibus venientibus ad animam
content_parts.summary: Devils arrive, mock the soul, enumerate its sins, and seize it for punishment.
notes: This section comprises ten eight-line stanzas in the source, but one XML LG spans encoded lines 433-448 and therefore yields nine LG elements.

content_parts.label: Iterum de anima dicente et clamante ad salvatorem suum
content_parts.summary: The soul cries to Christ; the narrator closes with an admonition to remember death and seek mercy.

related_works.title: Visio Philiberti (Noctis sub silentio tempore brumali)
notes: Scholarship treats the Middle English poem as a free adaptation within the tradition of the twelfth-century Latin verse debate, not a line-for-line translation. Historical attributions of the Latin poem to Walter Map, Bernard of Clairvaux, or Philibert are not authorship attributions for CME00101.

related_works.title: In a thestri stude I stod
notes: A distinct Middle English body-and-soul debate in the same wider Latin-derived tradition, not another witness of CME00101.

related_works.title: Un samedi par nuit
notes: A distinct Old French body-and-soul debate related through the wider tradition.

evidence.locator: IDG, BIBNO, and VID CME00101; TEXT LANG='enm'; poem heading through CLOSER and explicit
evidence.summary: The repository XML identifies CME00101 and supplies the complete encoded Royal-witness transcription used for title, language, structure, contents, and extent assertions.

evidence.locator: DIV1, DIV2, HEAD, LG, L, PB, MILESTONE, TEMPHEAD
evidence.summary: Element counting found one poem DIV1, eight dialogue/scene DIV2 elements, 66 LG elements, 536 L elements, and 16 PB elements. Section line totals are 136, 64, 56, 48, 48, 16, 32, 80, and 56. One LG contains 16 lines (encoded lines 433-448), explaining the difference from the source's 67 eight-line stanzas.

evidence.citation: Hermann Varnhagen, 'Zu mittelenglischen Gedichten: I-III,' Anglia 2 (1879): 225-255
evidence.locator: Section I, pp. 225-252; Royal MS 18 A X transcription, pp. 229-244; PDF pages 241-256 for printed pp. 229-244
evidence.summary: The immediate scholarly edition names Royal MS 18 A X as its base witness, prints the 536-line poem in 67 stanzas, explains its editorial interventions, and collates it with other witnesses. CME00101 reproduces only the Royal transcription, not the introduction or apparatus.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, record 605 (IMEV/NIMEV 351)
evidence.locator: work record and seven witness records
evidence.summary: DIMEV identifies the work by its incipit and title, classifies it as a body-and-soul debate/dialogue, describes its eight-line stanza form, lists seven manuscripts, and links Royal MS 18 A X to Varnhagen's pp. 229-244. Its French/macaronic and Royal-explicit fields contain record-level problems documented in this manifest rather than copied uncritically.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 1.0 dataset
evidence.summary: The versioned source dataset fixes the exact DIMEV record consulted and documents its CC BY 4.0 license and project responsibility.

evidence.citation: British Library, Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Royal MS 18 A X, record 040-002107426
evidence.summary: The current catalogue dates the vellum codex to the first half of the fifteenth century and identifies the poem at ff. 61v-66v as 67 eight-line stanzas. It records stanza IV in another hand on inserted f. 62, supplies the rubric, incipit, ending, and Latin explicit, and cites Varnhagen p. 229.

evidence.citation: National Library of Scotland, Auchinleck Manuscript, 'The Debate between the Body and the Soul' headnote
evidence.summary: The NLS scholarly project distinguishes the Auchinleck text from six other witnesses, dates Royal MS 18 A X about 1425-1450, gives its 67-stanza extent, and uses the established Middle English work title.

evidence.citation: Database of Middle English Romance, Oxford, Bodleian Library, MS Laud Misc. 108
evidence.locator: manuscript date, origin, and item at ff. 200v-203r
evidence.summary: The manuscript description dates Laud Misc. 108 to the late thirteenth or early fourteenth century, localizes it to the West Midlands, and locates the body-and-soul debate on ff. 200v-203r. This supports earliest-attestation facts, not the exact composition place.

evidence.citation: Jennifer A. Lorden, 'The Poetics of the Speaking Soul from Old to Middle English,' in Forms of Devotion in Early English Poetry: The Poetics of Feeling (Cambridge University Press, 2023)
evidence.summary: The chapter places 'Als I lay' and 'In a thestri stude' in the body-and-soul debate tradition derived from twelfth-century Latin verse and distinguishes the Middle English poems and Old French analogue as separate works.

open_questions.question: Can the poem's composition be dated or localized more narrowly than the early-fourteenth-century outer bound in England?
next_steps: Consult a current critical edition or dedicated stemmatic study of all seven witnesses.
Keep witness dialect and production-place evidence scoped to each manuscript rather than transferring it to the work.
notes: The date and region currently reported are bounded, evidence-backed cataloging statements, not claims of exact composition circumstances.
The Royal manuscript's holding in London and Varnhagen's publication in Halle are lineage facts, not composition places.
DIMEV's seven entries are manuscript witnesses of this work. The Latin Visio Philiberti and other vernacular body-and-soul debates are modeled separately as related works.
The XML's 66 LG elements should not be silently normalized to 67: it is an encoding fact caused by one 16-line LG, while 67 stanzas is the textual and manuscript-catalogue count.
